articles.title: Why We Choose NOT To Take The Step Towards Better
articles.excerpt: Fear and faith often sit on opposite ends of the same spectrum. Where fear says, "What if God doesn't?" -- faith says, "Even if He doesn't, I trust Him." The opposite of fear isn't bravery. It's trust.

articles.title: Not Cats No Cradle, No Regrets
articles.excerpt: As a son and now a father of three, I sometimes think about the moments we almost missed. The backyard baseball games. The jungle gyms. The walks that went nowhere in particular. Presence isn't a virtue -- it's a choice you make every single day.

articles.title: What Happened to My Friends?
articles.excerpt: You live a fulfilling life so far. You're in your mid-40s. You look back and realize you had all of these friends. Life gets busy. You build a family. You buy a home. And somewhere in there, the friendships quietly faded.

articles.title: The Art of Learning
articles.excerpt: When is the right time to pick up a new skill? I wanted to learn how to play a certain instrument, but I always felt like it was too late. Moments in our life are all relative to each other.

articles.title: Basketball Player / But Not a Real One
articles.excerpt: I have always loved the game. Jordan years. At the ripe age of 46, I started playing for real.

articles.title: Accepting Invitations
articles.excerpt: To speak. To connect. To hear a pitch. To partner. To mentor. Why I say yes -- and the three reasons that make it worth it.

articles.title: Brand -- Start by Looking Inward
articles.excerpt: It already exists. Clarity isn't about reinventing yourself -- it's about recognizing what's already unique. The most powerful brands don't get built from the outside in.

articles.title: Vision vs. Execution
articles.excerpt: The art and science of knowing when to dream and when to build. All mediums, all mindsets -- and why the gap between them is where most great ideas quietly disappear.

articles.title: Basketball Diaries
articles.excerpt: My obsession through the pandemic gave me the courage to play pick up basketball. This is that story, told one game at a time.

articles.title: What Jazz Taught Me About Trusting a Process You Can't Control
articles.excerpt: Jazz taught me to listen before I spoke, to trust the space between notes, and to find structure inside chaos. Turns out, faith works the same way.
articles.body:
I started playing jazz guitar in college. Before that, everything I knew about music was classical -- structured, precise, every note accounted for. You played what was on the page. Deviate and you were wrong.

Jazz broke all of that open.

My instructor at the time said something I've never forgotten. We were working through a standard, and I kept filling every measure -- runs, chords, motion. He stopped me mid-phrase and said, the notes you don't play are as important as the ones you do.

I didn't fully understand it then. I thought he was talking about technique.

He wasn't.

The space is where meaning lives

In jazz, silence isn't absence. It's tension. It's anticipation. It gives the next note somewhere to land. When you fill every measure, you're not playing music -- you're generating noise with good intentions.

The musicians I came to admire most were the ones who understood restraint. Miles Davis. Bill Evans. They could hold a room with a single note held a beat longer than expected. The space around the note was the point.

That lesson took years to move from my hands to the rest of my life.

Faith works the same way

There's a version of faith that looks like constant motion. Doing more. Saying more. Filling the silence with activity so it feels like trust. I lived in that version for a long time.

What I've come to understand is that faith -- real faith -- requires a willingness to sit in the space between the notes. Between the prayer and the answer. Between the step you took and the ground that hasn't appeared yet.

That space is uncomfortable. It doesn't feel like anything is happening. But that's exactly where the music is being made.

A process you can't control

The hardest part of improvisation isn't playing. It's listening. You have to trust your bandmates. You have to trust the structure underneath -- the changes, the tempo, the key. And then you have to let go of the outcome.

You can't control what the pianist does next. You can only respond honestly to what you hear.

I think about that a lot when I'm in a season of waiting. When the next thing hasn't shown itself yet. When the plan I had doesn't match the room I'm standing in.

Jazz taught me that the right response isn't to force the next note. It's to listen harder. To trust the structure. To believe that the rest of the band knows something you don't -- and that the song is still going.

The space isn't empty. It's full of what's coming next.
